title: Setting up a real nappy laundry system
subtitle: Choosing nappy types, setting the right wash cycle, and organising a rotation that fits around a baby's schedule
excerpt: Real nappies prevent 4,000-6,000 disposable nappies per child going to landfill. The laundry system behind a real nappy rotation is straightforward once the wash settings and rotation size are correct. A family of four with one baby needs 24-30 nappies in rotation and washes every 2-3 days at 40-60 degrees C. This covers nappy types, the wet bag versus nappy bucket debate, wash settings, and drying.
sourceNotes: The Nappy Alliance UK guidance on real nappy use and care. Real Nappy Week guidance (WRAP-funded). Cambridgeshire County Council real nappy incentive scheme washing guidance. Which? independent testing on nappy washing energy use.

content.text: A single baby gets through around 5,000 disposable nappies before potty training. Most go to landfill. Real nappies still need washing, but they move the environmental cost away from manufacturing and landfill and into water and energy instead. Studies found real nappies have a lower carbon footprint than disposables if washed at 60 degrees C or below and line-dried.
All-in-one (AIO): absorbent layer and waterproof outer sewn together. Easiest to use, closest to a disposable in operation. Longest drying time because the layers cannot be separated.
Pocket nappy: waterproof shell with a pocket; an absorbent insert is stuffed inside before use. Insert washes and dries separately. Fastest drying of the modern nappy types.

Two-part system: separate absorbent inner (prefold, fitted, or flat) plus a waterproof wrap. The wrap is reused across several inner changes if not soiled. The inner must be washed each use; the wrap every 2-3 uses.
Newborns: 10-12 changes per day. Washing every other day requires 20-24 nappies.
3-12 months: 6-8 changes per day. Washing every 2-3 days requires 18-24 nappies.
12 months and over: 4-6 changes per day. Washing every 3 days requires 15-20 nappies.
Laundry system: pre-wash, main wash, drying

Storage before washing: dry pail method (not in water). Place soiled nappies in a mesh bag inside a lidded bucket or nappy bin. A wet bag in a pail allows the bag to go directly into the washing machine without handling each nappy. Do not soak; soaking degrades elastics and fabrics and serves no hygiene benefit.
Solid waste: dispose of any solid material into the toilet before storing. A fleece liner placed inside the nappy makes this easier.
Pre-wash: cold rinse cycle (no detergent) before the main wash to flush out residues.

Main wash: 40-60 degrees C with a full dose of non-bio powder detergent. Powder performs better than liquid for nappy washing. Avoid fabric conditioner; it coats the fibres and reduces absorbency. Avoid plant-based or eco detergents in small doses; nappies need a full, effective wash.
Drum load: wash at 60-75% capacity. An under-loaded or overloaded machine does not agitate effectively.
Line dry whenever possible: extends nappy life, bleaches staining in sunlight (direct UV removes most stains), uses no energy.

Tumble drying at low heat is safe for most cotton and bamboo nappies. Avoid tumble drying PUL (polyurethane laminate) waterproof wraps and pockets; the heat delaminates the waterproof layer over time. Dry waterproof components at room temperature.

attrs.symptom: Nappies are leaking after a few months of use
attrs.cause: Detergent build-up (residue) or fabric conditioner has coated the fibres and reduced absorbency
attrs.fix: Strip wash: run nappies through a hot wash (60 degrees C) with no detergent, then rinse until no suds appear. If suds appear after several rinses, switch detergent. Never use fabric conditioner again on that nappy rotation.

attrs.cause: High-temperature tumble drying or soaking before washing is degrading the elastic
attrs.fix: Dry waterproof components at room temperature. Stop soaking. Some brands offer elastic replacement; contact the manufacturer.
